source_title: Q&A #3—Ambition, Narcissistic Parents, Addiction to Stress, Parenting Emotional Experiences, Emotional Fluidity

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: We had a lot of questions coming in. We have a survey up on Spotify. If you're listening to this on Spotify, you might see when you finish an episode that there's a way to ask us a question or suggest another topic. Others have been asking via Twitter, X, and other formats. So we've got a whole bunch of questions, and I'd love to see how many of those we could make it through and make this a Q&A episode.

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: Dima asks about ambition. Where does ambition come from, and how do you deal with the pressure it creates?

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: I don't know. I mean, I know that we're all born with some level of ambition. I suspect that neurologically speaking, that that level of ambition is different based on some neurochemical genetic brain construct that is unique in all of us. But I've never met anybody with zero ambition, right? They would just be not even eat and then die. So I think we're born with it is the short answer to where does ambition come from. I would say that ambition doesn't create pressure. Needing to be of value, thinking that who you are is tied to your success and your ambition, that would all create a lot of pressure. Just the raw ambition, it's like, I want to get up and walk out of bed. That's what ambition is.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: The pressure of ambition comes when you start attaching stories to it, such as, I'm no good if I don't. My identity requires that I, if I don't, the whole world is going to collapse. All that stuff, I think, is what creates the pressure. The ambition itself is almost the relief of the pressure. I heard a definition of stress recently. The definition was beautiful in the fact that it wasn't overwhelming stress based on everything you have to do. It was based on not doing it. If I am not acting on the thing that I want to be acting on, that there's a lot of stress that shows up in my system. The ambition is the impetus to move, so I don't see that as the stress. I think it's the stories that we tell ourselves or the ways that we convince ourselves that we're stuck that causes the stress.

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: Beth asks, Using emotion at people, what does that look like for emotions like sadness, anxiety, et cetera? Is love an emotion that gets used at people?

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: Absolutely. All of the above. I think you can constantly watch people who want other people to hold their fear and they'll come into a conversation and, Oh, I'm really, really worried about it. They're hoping that somebody else will also be really worried about it so that they're not alone in the worry. It's literally using your emotion to manage somebody else. You see this in offices all the time. Everybody's really anxious and they need everybody else to be anxious or they don't feel safe. Getting sad at people, anybody who's guilt tripped anybody else, sad at somebody is a prime guilt trip movement. If you look and you're like, Oh, if that person doesn't change their behavior at all, am I still okay? If the answer is no, then you know you're in that place of doing it at somebody.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: If it's love at somebody, it's not love, so there's that. But there's this thing that looks like love at somebody that is definitely like, I'm going to love you up so that you love me back or I'm going to love you up so that I can feel like a sense of connection that you don't particularly want to give me. All that stuff that you can use love for, but then it's not really love. But it looks like love and it might feel like love a little bit, but it isn't love.

paragraphs.speaker: brett
paragraphs.name: Brett Kistler
paragraphs.text: How do you apply the view principles when interacting with a narcissistic parent?

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: We all have a narcissism to us. The idea that there's somebody who's a narcissist and we're not is ridiculous. All of us have some way in which we are narcissistic. Narcissism is an inability to feel emotions. It's the best definition I've ever heard of it. I think it was Lohan who said it. He said, narcissism is the incapacity to feel emotions. Mentally, what I see is it's the part that thinks you're better and worse than somebody else. People who are narcissists very much think they're smarter than everybody or most people, but they also feel like there's something really wrong and broken with them.
